sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Larry, presents with a chronic history of back pain that has been ongoing for two years.
He reports a sharp stabbing pain in his back that radiates down to his right leg, specifically to his big toe, and is exacerbated by sitting.
He also experiences numbness and tingling in his right leg, particularly in the foot.
He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area and does not experience weakness or loss of control of his bladder or bowels.
He has tried physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past but has not found relief.
He has gained weight due to decreased activity level and is unable to participate in activities he enjoys, such as golf.
Past medical history:
The patient reports that he has no family members with spine conditions and has not smoked in the past 20 years.
He has a history of attempting to manage his back pain with physical therapy and pain medications.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has midline tenderness at the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
The supine straight leg test is positive, and there is decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results:
The MRI results show disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy, meaning that one of his disks is bulging out and pinching on nerves, causing the tingling in his leg.
The problem list is: Herniated disk with radiculopathy; Active.
Plan:
I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
The injection will be administered under fluoroscopy to target the exact area of pain.
This procedure is expected to be relatively quick and common, and the patient can expect to be out of the office within an hour.
The patient will be monitored to determine the effectiveness of the injection and the interval between subsequent injections will be adjusted accordingly.
Follow-up:
The patient will be scheduled for the first injection today, and if successful, subsequent injections will be scheduled as needed.
